evidence_quotes.quote: video game ownership and long-term access.
planned obsolescence of digital products, particularly online-only games or titles dependent on proprietary servers.
Require transparency at the point of sale about any time-limited access or server dependencies.

paragraphs.text: I would like to draw your attention to a growing concern within the digital entertainment sector, particularly regarding consumer rights in the context of video game ownership and long-term access. In this regard, I strongly urge you to review and consider the objectives of the Stop destroying Videogames initiative, which highlights serious issues around digital ownership and planned obsolescence in the gaming industry. An increasing number of major video game publishers have adopted business models that prioritize short-term profitability over consumer rights. These models often include the planned obsolescence of digital products, particularly online-only games or titles dependent on proprietary servers. In many cases, developers deliberately implement always-online requirements even for single-player or non-networked content not out of technical necessity, but as a strategic choice to ensure the game becomes unplayable once server support is withdrawn. This artificial dependency serves no meaningful purpose other than to facilitate control over the product lifecycle and to hasten its obsolescence, ultimately forcing consumers to repurchase similar content in newer formats. Once these servers are shut down or support is discontinued, the consumer is left with a non-functional product, regardless of whether they paid full price. This practice stands in stark contrast to consumer expectations in other product categories. When an individual purchases a physical item like a refrigerator or a television they expect to own that product indefinitely, with the ability to continue using it as long as it remains functional. There is no precedent in those markets for a manufacturer remotely disabling a product post-sale to force the purchase of a newer version. Yet this has become a normalized, albeit troubling, practice in the gaming sector. Such conduct is not only deeply anti-consumer but also undermines trust in digital marketplaces. It effectively erodes the concept of ownership and blurs the line between purchasing and leasing. When consumers buy a game, they believe they are acquiring a lasting product not renting access subject to arbitrary termination. To ensure digital fairness and uphold consumer protection standards, I urge the inclusion of legal provisions that: -Prevent companies from rendering purchased digital products non-functional without offering viable offline alternatives or other alternatives such as the ability for community ran servers -Require transparency at the point of sale about any time-limited access or server dependencies. -Enshrine the right to preserve and access legitimately purchased digital games, even if the original vendor discontinues support. -Consider digital game ownership equivalent to physical ownership, including post-sale usage rights. The Digital Fairness Act represents an important opportunity to restore balance between digital content creators and consumers. It should not enable or condone exploitative models that rely on digital disposability and undermine user rights. Thank you for your consideration.
